Match Analysis

Kerry v Tyrone

Possession-by-possession Analysis
7 July 2026
WINNERS
Kerry
2-25
FULL TIME
Kerry
by 4
Tyrone
0-27

Kerry's kickout masterclass meant they dominated possession despite being turned over 13 times. Tyrone were more wasteful in front of goal.

On kick-outs, Kerry retained 84% of their own restarts to Tyrone’s 68%. 

How the match was won

From possessions to points: the butterfly chart shows how each team translated the ball they had into scores. Kerry dominated possession (+6) but were caught for several turnovers. Tyrone were able to turn possessions to shots (+2), but were wasteful in front of goal compared with the Kingdom (65% vs.72%).  .

Reading it: where every possession originated — Kerry 45 to Tyrone’s 39. A longer bar means more ball.

1

How The Lead Changed

The running margin across all 84 possessions. Above the line, Kerry lead; below the line, Tyrone lead. Kerry led for most of the game but were just 1 point ahead for large parts of the second half. 

Half-time: Kerry 1-11 · Tyrone 0-12.

2

Spotlight: Key Phase of Play

Kerry went from 3 points down to two points up across just 5 possessions in the first half, capitalising on two Tyrone wides. This sequence was finished off by a crucial goa from David Clifford.

Kerry attack ↑
Tyrone attack ↓
Score
Wide / short
Kick-out restart
KER 1-06 – 0-07 TYR
How to read this

Reading it: each column is one possession. The chip on the halfway line shows how the ball was won, in the colour of the team that won it. The bold arrow traces the attack toward goal; scores and wides line up on the goal line. A turnover or short box stretches into the next column where the opponent’s possession runs on from it.

3

The Kick-Out Battle

Each team’s own kick-outs, by where the ball was won. A filled ring is retained; a grey ring is conceded. Kerry retained a remarkable 84% of their kickouts. This led to a net score of +23, with Tyrone scoring just 3 points of Kerry kickouts. 

Kick-out returns by length iNet score is the points a team scored directly from its own kick-outs minus the points conceded directly from them — counting only the possession that began with the kick-out. Short = ball won inside the own 45 m line; long = beyond it.

Where Possession Was Lost

Turnovers conceded, with each team attacking "up" the pitch. Kerry were turned over 13 times, conceding 10 points in the process.

Conceded directly from turnovers iConceded directly from turnovers is what the opposition scored in the possession immediately after winning the ball, as goals–points (e.g. 1‑03). Only open-play turnovers — lost in midfield or in the opposition half — are counted; shots dropped short or off the post are misses, not turnovers.

4

Scoring Chances

Every shot by location, each team attacking up the pitch. Marker size scales with expected score; diamonds are goals, hollow rings off target. Kerry were more accurate, converting 72% of their chances into scores and getting 2 goals in the process.

Player Analysis

Click on any player to show their main involvements, green for positives (scores, turnovers won, goalkeeper kick-outs won) and red for negatives (wides, shots dropped short or off the post, turnovers lost, kick-outs lost). Dylan Geaney and David Clifford shone up front for Kerry with Darren McCurry scoring a remarkable 10 points for Tyrone, despite coming off the bench in the second half.
